data.title: Corporate Stock Buybacks - Do They Affect Markets?
data.description:
Fisher Investments recently wrote an interesting article asking whether corporate stock buybacks affect markets. Here is their conclusion:

“Yes and no? Stocks move on supply and demand. Stock buybacks, where a company buys and takes shares off the market, theoretically reduce supply. They can also raise earnings per share, thus rewarding shareholders. So, all else equal and on paper, stock buybacks are bullish. But reality, as always, is more complicated. Buybacks are just one factor affecting supply. There are others, and demand matters, too. They may not reduce supply if they merely offset secondary issuances, like employee stock awards. Often, buybacks merely ‘sterilize’ new issuance. Other negative (or less bullish) fundamental factors might matter more in pricing, lowering demand even as supply shrinks. So buybacks are a factor, but not the factor.“

While the statement is mostly correct, I am unsure if they looked at the actual impact that corporate stock buybacks have on the market. We have discussed this topic and the past misstatements of corporate stock buybacks. Here is a listing for more background.

  1. They are not a return of capital to shareholders; dividends are.
  2. Corporate stock buybacks are the worst use of cash.
  3. It is a benefit that almost entirely benefits corporate insiders.

But, without rehashing the many problems of corporate stock buybacks, let’s focus on these transactions’ impact on the overall market.

As of May 2025, corporate stock buyback authorizations are on track to eclipse $1.35 trillion this year, with more than $1 trillion executed. This will exceed any other year in the market since the turn of the century. Such should be unsurprising with Apple (AAPL) announcing an additional $100 billion and Google (GOOGL) adding another $70 billion to their programs (those two programs will account for 12% of the total alone).

The data should lead one to question why corporate stock buybacks have grown steadily since the turn of the century. Such is particularly the case when the overreliance on buybacks at non-accretive valuations to boost stock prices has become commonplace. Such a statement undermines the fallacy that corporate stock buybacks are solely a return of capital to shareholders. For example, Apple’s $110 billion buyback plan in 2024 raised questions among some investors about whether the company focused too much on immediate stock price increases rather than on investments that could drive long-term value. That statement should not be overlooked, given that 5-year annualized revenue growth has been flat since 2018. (Chart courtesy of SimpleVisor.com)

If corporate stock buybacks are not a significant factor in increasing stock prices, why do companies engage in them so heavily? Why not just let market dynamics carry the load? The reason is simplistic to understand.

“Corporate executives give several reasons for stock buybacks but none of them has close to the explanatory power of this simple truth: Stock-based instruments make up the majority of their pay and in the short-term buybacks drive up stock prices.” – Financial Times.

So, how much of a factor are buybacks?

Are Buybacks An Important Factor

It is a pretty easy task to see whether or not corporate stock buybacks influence stock prices. As we penned last year, the impact of buybacks extends beyond individual companies. Since 2000, net corporate buybacks have accounted for 100% of the equity market’s net asset purchases — a reflection of the diminished participation from pensions, mutual funds, and individual investors:

  • Net Flow: +$5.2 trillion
  • Pensions & Mutual Funds: –$2.7 trillion
  • Households & Foreign Investors: +$2.4 trillion
  • Corporations (Buybacks): +$5.5 trillion

In other words, without corporate stock buybacks, the stock market would be roughly 30% lower today.

“The chart below via Pavilion Global Markets shows the impact stock buybacks have had on the market over the last decade. The decomposition of returns for the S&P 500 breaks down as follows:“

  • 6.1% from multiple expansions (21% at Peak),
  • 57.3% from earnings (31.4% at Peak),
  • 9.1% from dividends (7.1% at Peak), and
  • 27% from share buybacks (40.5% at Peak)

Yes, buybacks are that important.

However, to Fisher’s question directly, there is more than just a minor correlation between corporate stock buybacks and the market. The chart below overlays the 4-week change in stock buybacks versus the 4-week change in the S&P 500 index. It is worth noting that before 1982, the SEC considered share buybacks an illegal form of market manipulation. (In 1982, the SEC adopted Rule 10b-18, which provided "safe harbor" from liability of market manipulation. In other words, the SEC recognizes that buybacks manipulate the financial markets but provided a “shield” to corporations.)

The chart above is complex due to the large amount of data. The chart below is from 2021 to present, where changes to buybacks (increase or decrease) significantly impact changes to stock prices. It is worth noting that the nearly 20% decline in April was exacerbated by the sharp reversal in buyback activity and vice versa.

While Fisher suggests that buybacks have little to do with market movements, a high correlation exists between the 4-week percentage change in buybacks and the stock market. More importantly, since the act of share repurchases provides a buyer for those shares, the .85 correlation between the two suggests this is more than just a casual relationship.

But yes, Fisher is correct, other factors support higher asset prices.

Buybacks Affect More Than Just Prices

In 2023, Jason Zweig penned an article for the WSJ stating:

“Over the past five years, according to S&P Dow Jones Indices, big U.S. companies have spent $3.9 trillion repurchasing their own stock. Buybacks are neither bad nor good. They are simply a tool. Just as you can use a hammer either to build a house or knock one down, buybacks are useful in the right corporate hands and dangerous in the wrong ones.“ – Jason Zweig, WSJ

That is a fair statement. The impact of share buybacks is vital to manufacturing earnings growth, since we measure earnings on a per-share basis. In other words, if you reduce the number of shares outstanding, corporate earnings “per share” improve, as shown below.

As discussed previously, the annual rate of change in earnings growth is one of the best predictors of forward stock market returns.

However, investors must be cautious about understanding the impact of buybacks on earnings when investing in companies. As Warren Buffett noted:

Finally, an important warning: Even the operating earnings figure we favor can easily be manipulated by managers who wish to do so. Such tampering is often considered sophisticated by CEOs, directors and their advisors. Reporters and analysts embrace its existence as well. Beating ‘expectations’ is heralded as a managerial triumph. That activity is disgusting. It requires no talent to manipulate numbers: Only a deep desire to deceive is required. ‘Bold, imaginative accounting,’ as a CEO once described his deception to me, has become one of the shames of capitalism.“

Why would CEOs want to manipulate earnings? Unsurprisingly, a WSJ survey of CFOs found that 93% pointed to “influence on stock price” and “outside pressure” as the reasons for manipulating earnings figures.

Of course, one misnomer is that corporate CEOs execute buybacks when they believe the stock is undervalued. However, the reality is quite the opposite, and they tend to execute share repurchases when their current optimism is elevated. When share prices decline, and buybacks could be done at accretive prices, there is little incentive to do so.

Conclusion

The evidence is clear: corporate stock buybacks are not a marginal force in markets — they are central to the mechanics of price inflation. When buybacks account for the entire net demand for equities over the last two decades, it’s hard to argue they’re simply "a" factor. They aren’t just reducing supply on paper — they are the demand. Without them, equity valuations would look very different.

But what’s more concerning is the why. Despite the popular narrative that buybacks return capital to shareholders, the data and behavior of corporate management tell a different story. Buybacks overwhelmingly inflate earnings per share and boost short-term stock prices, which are tied directly to executive compensation. That incentive skews the timing and intent of buyback programs away from long-term value creation and toward short-term financial engineering.

To Fisher’s credit, markets are complex. Demand, sentiment, interest rates, and macroeconomic factors all matter. However, dismissing buybacks as one variable among many overlooks just how much they dominate equity flows. Their influence is measurable, intentional, and reinforced by corporate leadership’s financial incentives.

The question for investors is not whether buybacks matter — they do. The question is whether they’re being used to create real value or mask its absence

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Get Into GitLab
data.description:
Thesis

This article focuses on GitLab (GTLB) a smaller SaaS company that may interest investors with high-risk tolerances. The article explains what GitLab does, its “special sauce”, operating metrics and evidence that it’s executing well with artificial intelligence. I also focus on a few unique risks and mitigants. Lastly, I seek to value the company using specialized metrics such as the Rule of 40.

Introduction

A while back, I wrote an article “Screening for the best SaaS companies” in which I presented and recommended several enterprise-software companies. Those of you that are familiar with the space will likely recognize and agree that these are true leaders. However, people often ask me about the other six dozen names that rarely appear in the financial press. I typically give the same answer I give to those investing in cryptocurrencies, which is that you should avoid smaller names unless you have a high-risk tolerance, already own the largest companies, have done your own research and want to shift your portfolio up the “efficient frontier.”

Adding GitLab to a portfolio’s Efficient Frontier

GitLab is one such name as it’s smaller and riskier than the better-known SaaS names. This higher risk can be determined by examining GTLB’s beta and revenue scale (<$1Bn). A stock’s Standard Deviation or Beta are considered measures of risk (X-axis) in the above chart.

Business description

I often find SaaS companies difficult to understand, so I will explain GitLab’s business in layman’s terms. The company is a digital workspace that helps teams (especially in large enterprises) safely organize and work together on projects (mostly software). GitLab provides a central repository to code and keep track of changes. Users can even make copies of the project or carve-outs to “play in a sandbox.” Once a user is finished with a software module, GitLab can automatically test it to ensure it’s working properly.

There’s also a project component of GitLab that’s similar to what I described in my SA article on monday.com (MNDY). For example, GitLab provides “scrum boards” in which a team leader can assign & track tasks, timelines, milestones, documentation and charting & reporting. The key difference between GitLab and Monday.com is that MNDY is more general while GitLab is marketed to more technical software-intensive teams.

Industry Size & Growth

The global DevOps software market is expected to grow from $11.5Bn in 2023 to $66Bn by 2033. This equates to a CAGR of over 19%. The Generative AI niche within this market is$2Bn and growing at a 38% CAGR.

Largest players are the Mag-7 (Microsoft (MSFT)/GitHub, Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN)) and more focused companies such as Atlassian (TEAM) JFrog (FROG) Harness (privately owned) and to a lesser degree Monday.com. Large horizontal SaaS companies such as Salesforce (CRM) also have DevOp businesses.

Business Position

GitLab rates very high in Gartner’s Magic Quadrant for DevOps Software, but a bit lower in G2’s ranking (chart below), especially amongst larger corporations.

Management Rating

I typically favor founder-led SaaS companies, which was the case for GitLab until this year when Bill Staples took over this year for “Sid” who stepped down. GitLab’s culture is highly rated by both Comparably (see below) and Glassdoor. However, note that Mr. Staples’ approval rating (54%) is troublingly low on Glassdoor. Another red-flag is that there’s a new CRO (Chief Revenue Officer) so the marketing teams may witness higher turnover as the CRO changes strategy. For example, if the CRO decides to shift from a “self-serve” model to a dedicated sales team targeting large corporations, GitLab’s 91% gross profit margin could be at risk.

Secret Sauce

Every company in the SaaS space talks about integrating Artificial Intelligence (“AI”), but GitLab seems to be executing better. Rather than just using an AI copilot to summarize meetings and generate issue descriptions, GitLab uses AI in an integrative way. In fact, GitLab has several tools and agents for all of the testing, etc. that I described under the Business Description.

GitLab’s recent quarter (4Q ’25 ended January) slightly missed estimates, especially FY ’26 guidances, which was on the conservative side (probably related to the new CEO and CRO). A discussion of 4Q is out of scope to this article, but I do recommend reading Gary Alexander’s piece.

I see anecdotal evidence that GitLab has staying power with customers such as:

  • the number of customers that are doing large deals (>$100k) is growing faster than its total customer base (“Base Customers” on chart)
  • It’s AI tool, called GitLab Duo, has been gaining traction amongst customers.
  • “cRPO” revenues are growing at a strong rate, meaning expected, but unrecognized revenues are growing at a healthy-clip compared to historical revenue growth.
  • The more expensive "Ultimate" subscription plan, which is a comprehensive package offered to large organizations, is selling well and is now accounting for 50% of ARR (annualized recurring revenue) compared to 46% three quarters earlier.
  • Net Dollar Retention has begun to stabilize at 123% (bar-chart) and is above its peers
  • Amazon Web Services has expanded its relationship with GTLB including AWS’ bundled offering of GitLab Duo

Risks

GitLab is a smaller company compared to its direct competitors such as Microsoft (MSFT) which owns GitHub and Atlassian (TEAM). It also has lower and inconsistent Free Cash Flow margins and “Rule of 40” numbers compared to those companies. These are backwards-looking metrics and I expect they will improve going forward. For example, the FCF margin in the chart below has been very volatile and the Rule of 40 was just 20% during the TTM period. However, as SaaS companies age, their business model’s Operating Leverage typically allows them to reach that 40% hurdle-rate.

One of the reasons why GitLab’s Rule of 40 is so low is due to its young age (11 years in total including about 4 years as a publicly traded company. Again, the Rule of 40 numbers age well - like a fine-wine. If GitLab surpasses 40%, it’ll be ranked in the top one-third of all SaaS companies, albeit at the bottom of that sub-list (see bar-chart).

Valuation Analysis

The best method to value SaaS companies is examining EV/NTM Revenues (i.e., EV to forward Revenues). Analysts’ estimates for GitLab’s revenue growth approximate mid-20s percentage growth and most estimates have been close to that mean. The less certain estimates pertain to FCF Margin, of which will determine whether GitLab reaches the Rule of 40 hurdle rate. If it does, then I believe GTLB is currently trading at a discount to the Regression Line in the first chart below (EV multiple vs. Rule of 40). I also examined GTLB’s EV multiple vs. forward revenue growth rate, and it too, trades at a discount (second chart below).

Conclusion

I recommend investors who are looking to shift their portfolio up the Efficient Frontier to purchase shares of GTLB (rated “ Strong Buy”). The shares could rise sharply if management continues executing and if global liquidity (e.g., M2 Money Supply) continues expanding. There is also the chance that Amazon could acquire GitLab given its growing relationship with Amazon AWS.

data.title: Prologis: Q1 2025 Performance Delivered 4 Reasons To Buy More
data.description:
Prologis (PLD) remains one of my favorite REITs to invest in. I like collecting leaders of respective markets in my portfolio and PLD is an absolute giant of the logistic real estate market covering 5,900 buildings leased to 6,500 customers. It has $207B of assets under management and its properties cover 1.3B sq. ft. across 20 countries. Quite overwhelming, isn't it?

I've been bullish on Prologis since the first time I covered it. It's been some time since I last covered it (September 2024) and a lot has happened during that time - especially in terms of continued struggles of the industrial market and the risk of trade war escalation.

With the Q1 2025 results published, a breakthrough on the trade war front, and increasingly positive supply-to-demand developments in the market, I decided to take another look at it. Please feel free to read my previous coverages (here's the link).

Prologis Just Delivered Solid Q1 2025 Results - There Are Four Reasons To Keep Buying

Reason #1: Prologis Outperformed Its Own Expectations

As the company's CFO, Tim Arndt, mentioned during the most recent Earnings Call - PLD outperformed their own expectations in several measures. The core FFO per share amounted to $1.42, beating the management's expectations. While the average occupancy throughout the quarter amounted to 94.8%, Q1 2025 ended with 95.2% occupancy. Despite a 70 bps decrease since the end of 2024, this metric also exceeded management's expectations.

What's crucial for investors hesitant about investing in REITs with nearly 19x P/FFO multiple (on forward-looking basis) is the dynamic that PLD can increase its rent and let me tell you - it's no joke. As a reminder, market rents within the industrial niche tend to grow much more dynamically than rents following escalators embedded within lease contracts. As a result, when a given lease agreement is terminated - PLD can negotiate meaningful rent bumps, which amounted to ~32% on a cash basis during Q1 2025.

Although Prologis (almost fully) maintained its 2025 guidance despite some potential to increase it, I have some positive news that could result in PLD outperforming its own expectations once again. Before I mention that, let's take a look at the management's comment about PLD's guidance:

Turning to our guidance, as mentioned, our first quarter results and outlook would have called for tightening and increase of guidance, including earnings. Given the circumstances, however, we've opted to hold most areas unchanged with the main exception in capital deployment. We are reducing our development start guidance at our share to $1.5 billion to $2 billion, which reflects a reduction in our expectations for spec development until visibility improves. Our combined contribution and disposition guidance is also decreasing to a range of $400 million to $1 billion, again, reflecting uncertainty in both the capital markets and the fundraising environment. In turn, we are reducing our development gain guidance to a range of $100 million to $250 million. We are also increasing our G&A guidance to a new range of $450 million to $470 million, in part due to the impact on capitalization from the lower development activity.

Outside of this, all other areas of our prior guidance are unchanged with core FFO including net promote expense to still range between $5.65 and $5.81 per share and core FFO excluding net promote expense to range between $5.70 and $5.86 per share.

Naturally, the two main risk factors accompanying PLD are oversupply of the market (I'll cover that in my next point) and the risk of trade war escalation. Luckily, China and the U.S. reached a "truce" in Geneva. That makes me quite optimistic about future negotiations, and I consider the risk of economic downturn and global trade disruption as much less material than last month. Can that still change? Of course. Still, any volatility around that should have a near-term nature and wouldn't derail PLD's long-term game plan. Could it impact 2025? Yes, but if you aim to hold on to PLD longer - don't be too obsessed with that.

The U.S. will cut extra tariffs it imposed on Chinese imports in April this year to 30% from 145% and Chinese duties on U.S. imports will fall to 10% from 125% for the next 90 days, the two sides said on Monday.

Reason #2: The Industrial Market Keeps Showing More Signs Of Upcoming Improvement

Prologis, and many other industrial REITs, faced unfavorable supply-to-demand relationship during the last couple of years, beginning with mid-2022. How has that happened? The booming post-COVID industry took too many investment initiatives and created too much industrial space, leading to the broad market oversupply. That resulted in a worse negotiating position for landlords, making the ability to grow rent weaker.

That tendency of a gradually increasing oversupply of the market is showcased (in the U.S.) by continuously growing vacancy rate (up to 6.9% as of Q1 2025) and robust net completions often exceeding $100m sq. ft. during the last several quarters, reduced the negotiating position with tenants and led to oversupply of the market. In fact, the vacancy rate reached another peak with struggling net absorption (unfortunately it decreased compared to Q4 2024).

Luckily, as you can see in the chart above (the one on the right), the industrial construction starts are currently as low as they used to be in the mid-2010s. Q1 2025 delivered positive data regarding continued decrease in construction starts on a quarter-to-quarter basis. That major drop (when compared to 2022-2023 period) hints a potential cool-off of the market and a potential turnaround from oversupply to undersupply given (still relevant) demand drivers, such as demography, cyclical spending increase, e-commerce development, and improving financial stance of consumers.

As a leading distribution partner for top-tier global brands, Prologis is set to capitalize on these trends. I also believe that the current oversupply of the market will likely shift into undersupply quite soon.

Reason #3: Prologis Is A Gem For Dividend Investors

PLD's current dividend yield amounts to ~3.75%, which may not seem like a lot to some investors, but don't let that discourage you. Take a look at PLD's DPS CAGR recorded during the 2019–2024 period. It not only exceeded 12% but also outperformed other logistic REITs, S&P 500 REITs, S&P Index, and the REIT sector as a whole.

On top of that, PLD showcases one of the best balance sheets in the entire REIT sector. Its A-rated fortress is supported by a reasonable leverage, high 6.8x fixed charge coverage, outstanding (by industrial REITs' standards) weighted average maturity term, and solid debt-to-adj. EBITDA ratio of 4.9x.

Prologis has a long-standing track record of supporting operations of leading global players across several industries - with a well-diversified tenant structure as its top 10 customers only represent 15% of the total portfolio's rent. The diversification combined with a top-tier financial stance support PLD's liquidity, risk mitigation, and thus - dividend safety, which should be especially attractive to investors seeking reliable income sources.

Reason #4: Prologis Has A Few More Growth Catalysts Outside Of Its Core Business, Building On Its Unmatched Scale And Industrial Platform

PLD has growth catalysts even outside of its core business. Not to mention its $41B bank of land - Prologis also transforms its vast property network into future-proof facilities characterized by energy efficiency, possibility of fleet electrification (with EV charging), solar panels installed, and battery storage.

On top of that, Prologis capitalizes on the growing data center demand, generating even higher yields than in its warehouse business (7.5-10%). The demand in the U.S. is set to grow, as we can see significant investments from leading tech giants each earnings season.

Just during the last quarter (Q1 2025), Prologis realized tremendous progress on that front, expanding its capacity by 400 megawatts:

In our data center business, 400 megawatts of power has moved to our advanced stage category upon agreement with utilities for projects in a Tier 1 market. We now total 2 gigawatts in this category alongside our 1.4 gigawatts of power, which is already fully secured. Our conversations with utilities and hyperscalers alike have been very productive and we will have start activity to report for the second quarter.

Despite All That - Prologis Remains Undervalued

Prologis has everything going for it. Leadership position within a highly attractive market accompanied by several secular growth drivers. Resilience to short-to-mid-term headwinds. Strong negotiating position with leading global brands and a diversified tenant structure. Fortress-like balance sheet. Solid track record of dividend growth and value creation. Several growth catalysts outside of its core business allowing PLD to capitalize on its overwhelming scale and global property network.

Moreover, the management keeps a disciplined approach to day-to-day operations and investments, ensuring reliability of their platform and capitalization on the "economies of scale". They prove PLD's strength by exceeding their own expectations during uncertain times - Q1 2025 was a positively surprising update.

Despite all that, PLD trades at quite a modest (by industrial REITs' standards) forward-looking P/FFO ratio of ~18.8x. For those accustomed to retail-oriented REITs, the multiple may seem high, but please keep in mind that market rent growth within the industrial niche substantially exceeded the contractual growth. That allows industrial REITs to bump rents upon lease expirations or renewals, growing their financials much more dynamically.

While Wall Street Analysts expect an over 10% upside resulting solely from stock price increase within the following 1-year period, I believe it still underappreciates Prologis. With all that said, and keeping in mind the risk factors ahead of PLD (discussed earlier), I consider PLD an attractive risk-to-reward opportunity and expect double-digit total returns. Therefore, PLD is a "buy" for me as I continue reinvesting. Cheers!

data.title: The Cheesecake Factory: Upside Is Not Attractive Despite Positive Fundamental Momentum
data.description:
Investment action

I give a hold rating for The Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), as I think the near-term upside is already priced in. While I’m encouraged by the strong 1Q25 margin performance, steady SSSG, and momentum in new store development, the stock is already trading slightly above its historical valuation range. Unless we see a step-up in growth or upward revisions to earnings estimates, I think the upside is not attractive.

Company description

CAKE owns and operates a portfolio of restaurants. As of 1Q25, it has 358 company-owned restaurants in the US and Canada, including 215 The Cheesecake Factory locations, 45 North Italia restaurants, 40 Flower Child eateries, and 51 Fox Restaurant Concepts [FRC] units. Additionally, there are 33 Cheesecake Factory restaurants operated internationally under licensing agreements. The company also runs two bakery facilities that produce its signature cheesecakes and other baked goods for its various channels.

1Q25 Performance Showed Resilience

CAKE's 1Q25 results did way better than I had imagined, given the current macro backdrop in the US. Revenue came in at $927.2 million, representing 4% y/y growth, marking the 4th consecutive quarter of mid-single-digit growth. Same-store-sales growth [SSSG] was up 1% y/y, which may not seem very bullish on face value, but against the current macro, bad weather, and LA wildfires in 1Q25, I think this is a very solid performance. Management did acknowledge that the environment feels “a touch softer” than it did a few months ago, but given that SSSG still held steady at low single digits (not dipping to negative), it speaks very well of CAKE brand strength. What really stood out to me was the margin performance. Four-wall margin expanded by 140 bps y/y to 17.4%, driving restaurant-level margins to 15.9% (90 bps y/y expansion). This solid margin expansion was driven by various factors such as food cost control, productivity gains, wage management, and improved staff and manager retention. More importantly, adj. EPS grew 27% y/y to $0.93—marking the sixth straight quarter of >20% growth.

Store Development and Brand Strength Drive Growth

I see a good chance that CAKE can continue sustaining this mid-single-digit growth trend, or even potentially accelerate growth, and the underlying reason is because management is reaccelerating the development pipeline.

Management described the 1Q25 start as “excellent,” with eight new openings—3 North Italia, 3 Flower Child, and 2 under the FRC. In percentage terms, that is ~5.7% y/y increase in units (excluding international licenses). This means that CAKE is very well on track to hit their FY25 goal of opening up to 25 new restaurants, especially with another eight openings already lined up for 2Q25. Effectively, CAKE has covered more than 50% of its target, which means there is a good chance it can beat its own guidance. The thing to note here is that CAKE is not opening for the sake of meeting guidance. Early performance has been promising in these new locations. For instance, North Italia’s new locations, including the first in Salt Lake City, did above-average average unit volume (AUV), and Flower Child is also seeing solid demand, according to management. I take these demand levels as a validation that there is latent demand for CAKE’s product.

Beyond new store growth, I think CAKE’s Cheesecake Rewards program is becoming an increasingly important part of the growth story. Management noted that member acquisition is already running ahead of expectations, and more importantly, guest feedback has been strong—member satisfaction scores are reportedly over-indexing. That tells me this program isn’t just growing, but it is actually resonating with guests.

The more compelling thing here is CAKE's shift from broad-based testing to a more behavior-driven strategy. Specifically, CAKE is now tailoring offers based on individual member activity and preferences, and I totally support this shift since people are more responsive to offers that feel relevant. This shift has apparently worked since early engagement metrics suggest this personalized approach is driving stronger results. My view here is that this reward program could become a real traffic engine over time. Especially in a softer consumer environment, having a direct and highly targeted channel to drive repeat visits is a big win.

Balance sheet strength

To facilitate CAKE's growth strategy, a strong balance sheet is needed. Thankfully, CAKE has one. In 1Q25, CAKE refinanced its debt by issuing $575 million of 2% convertible notes due 2030. From that, it used ~$558 million in net proceeds to retire $276 million of its 2026 convertible notes, repurchase 2.4 million shares, and fully pay down its revolver. The end result is that CAKE now has $135 million in cash and another $366 million in unused revolver capacity, which basically gives it >$500 million in available liquidity. CAKE did incur a $15.9 million pre-tax charge from the note retirement, but I think that is a relatively small price to pay for a cleaner capital structure. This financial move also frees up room to keep returning capital to shareholders. In fact, management repurchased 2.6 million shares in 1Q25 and maintained the $0.27 quarterly dividend.

Valuation

The unfortunate thing here is I believe the market has priced in the upside over the next two years. As per my model, CAKE is trading near where it should. My model assumes CAKE will meet management's FY25 consolidated sales guidance of $3.76 billion (implies mid-single-digit percentage growth) sustain this mid-single-digit percentage revenue growth through FY27, delivering ~$4.145 billion in total revenue. I do expect margin to expand modestly as CAKE should enjoy high incremental margin as the new stores ramp up to maturity. Assuming a 5bps improvement per quarter (using FY25 guidance implied expansion as a baseline), I got to CAKE delivering ~$200 million in net income. Here is the issue: CAKE is already valued at 14x forward PE, which is ~1x above the high end of its trading band (10x to 13x). Even if I assume that CAKE can sustain multiples at this level, I only see a 6% upside over two years. This is not attractive.

Risks

A downside risk for CAKE is the pressure on consumer discretionary spending from a softer macroeconomic environment. This would potentially worsen traffic trends and impact SSSG. This would cause CAKE to miss its guidance and unable to sustain mid-single-digit growth. Furthermore, slower-than-expected scaling of newer concepts like North Italia and Flower Child would delay the pace of margin expansion.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for CAKE. SSSG showed resilience despite a softer macro, margins are expanding, and new unit growth is progressing well with encouraging early results. The loyalty program and balance sheet moves also support the long-term growth story. That said, with the stock already trading at 14x forward earnings—above the high end of its historical range—I think most of this good news is reflected in the price.

data.title: Why Warner Bros. Discovery May Already Be Winning Before The Summer Season Even Begins
data.description:
If you were to look at the entertainment industry at the start of the year and pick who you thought would be the most buzzed about studio entering the summer season – odds are Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) was not going to be your pick.

The studio whose 2024 credits include notable misses Furiosa, Horizons and The Flash has not had the easiest path to profitability in recent years. From the failed AT&T years to the “stream first” experiment of 2021 to the rocky launch (and re-launch - and now somehow re-re-launch) of HBO MAX, shareholders have had no shortage of “shake their head” moments.

Yes, it hasn’t been all bad – Dune, Barbie and virtually its entire horror slate has over-indexed in tremendous ways, but it is hard to escape that overarching sense of unevenness around the studio. However, we knew WBD would eventually begin to turn it around, but usually the expectation is that would come during the summer box office season, not the traditionally dead Spring run-up.

And yet here we are.

So how did WBD get here, and can it sustain itself?

First, as always, some background.

I’m not going to harp too much on what went wrong for WBD in past years as I’ve written a lot on it in the past. But the short version is you could do a few “We Didn’t Start The Fire” style verses listing out their recent flops and misfires.

The saving grace though is while WBD was finding its way, other divisions of WBD were picking up the slack – chief among them HBO, which has always played that support role rather well.

This week's name-carousel game notwithstanding.

But to be clear - regardless of whatever you want to call its streaming service - WBD’s backbone has long been movies and when you think of them, you think of movies, so when that division is having problems, you can see why investors may worry.

And it did look like 2025 would be a repeat of 2024 at the start.

While its last earning period did have a few bright spots, a loss in film revenue stood out to investors. It wasn’t exactly a surprise as the studio ended 2024 on a down note courtesy of whatever Joker: Folie a Duex was (audiences still aren’t sure).

Things then continued to spiral as its first two 2025 movies (The Alto Knights and Mickey 17) followed suit in under-performing.

Summer seemingly could not get here soon enough.

While investors were getting used to having to grin and bear it while riding the storm out until Superman swooped into theaters, something unexpected happened – well actually two unexpected things happened.

A Minecraft Movie and Sinners opened.

A Minecraft Movie was never expected to be the movie it became. After seeing Warcraft go down in flames at the box office, analysts were mixed whether another big gaming franchise of that size and scale could make the jump to the big screen successfully.

And the early returns on Minecraft seemed to indicate the answer was no.

With a Rotten Tomatoes score of 53% and a collection of reviews that at times seemed more designed to mock the movie than inform audiences about it, it looked liked the movie was quickly being written off.

Then it opened.

The movie collected a staggering $163 million in its opening weekend. Not only was that the year’s highest opening (yes, I know it was a low bar) but the best for a video game adaptation ever.

Call it a pent-up demand for a family movie or the appeal of leads Jack Black and Jason Momoa, or the fact the movie was everywhere in the run-up with over 40 sponsored deals with companies, but this was a huge win and surprise.

It also shows reviews don’t matter for movies like this one.

Something you’d think the industry would have learned by now.

Yet after the under-performance of Captain America: Brave New World, the industry was on edge and didn’t see Minecraft as the movie to push them back from the brink.

As it turned out though WBD wasn’t done yet.

Two weeks later the studio released Sinners…a higher budget ($90 million), high-concept horror film that reunited Michael B. Jordan and his Creed director Ryan Coogler.

The movie was just as under-estimated.

Despite the fact Jordan and Coogler are bona-fide industry names and heavy-weights, many wrote the movie off. Then came the $48 million debut and everyone started paying attention.

Globally the movie earned over $60 million in its first weekend and has since crossed the $200 million mark (domestically), a feat not achieved by an original (i.e. non-IP) movie in eight years.

Sinners is also unique in that Coogler’s deal with WBD specifies that after 25 years the movie’s rights revert back to him. It’s an aspect that has caught the attention of many analysts and studio executives who are worried about what that could mean going forward for future industry deals.

Yet, many forget we’ve seen this before – Quentin Tarantino previously had a 30-year deal in place with Miramax and the arrangements made to lure players like James Cameron and Christopher Nolan to a studio are very director-friendly.

Whether they will own the film in the end or not, they are getting paid as if they do.

Sinners hit different though because it was very low-profile, and it seemingly came out of nowhere.

It also led some to question its profitability. Even after a hot start, there were a few quickly trying to cool the movie off - with some going so far as suggesting questions that are rooted in the same type of discrimination that is prevalent in the film’s plot.

It’s a fascinating (and disturbing) turn of events that you can read more about here. Combined with Coogler’s ownership deal, it suggests a sad scenario where part of the industry was seemingly trying to take the air out of Sinners’ impressive haul when in fact it should be celebrated.

For WBD, regardless of what it had to give up to Coogler to get the movie, the trade-off was worth it. Sinners paired with Minecraft marked the first time one studio has had two movies land at #1 and #2 in the box office – and gross over $40 million in one weekend – since 2009.

And it still has its summer slate featuring Superman, the Brad Pitt led F1 and three horror films (two of which are franchises) in the pipeline.

It is important to note, though that WBD’s problem hasn’t always been having hits, it’s been sustaining that momentum for an extended period of time. And that could still be in play here, as you can see a drop-off in films past the summer season - at present, the studio only has three movies slated for the Fall and none past October.

Yes, this could be a big year for the studio, but what will really make investors excited is to see if they can keep that buzz going. For now though shareholders are entitled to enjoy the win because its been a while.

data.title: Dave: Excellent Opportunity To Sell And Take Profits
data.description:
The valuation of Dave (NASDAQ:DAVE) has passed from approximately $500 million to $2.42 billion in over six months. Despite a strong backdrop and guidance from the company, in my opinion, this appreciation is an opportunity to sell the stock. The company's share price has skyrocketed to the point that the appealing conditions are no longer attractive for an investor to hold the shares or start a position.

Company Description

Dave's business consists of underwriting short-term loans of an average of $192 with a typical term of one to two weeks. Don't get me wrong, although I believe they are a "sell," this is a Fintech that has numerous advantages compared to conventional banks. For example, Dave does not use FICO scores to analyze credit risk. Instead, they use AI to analyze customers' risk profiles based on real-time data on income, spending, and employment. Furthermore, they have an inexpensive CAC of approximately $18, which is substantially lower than the $500 of legacy banks. Coupled with a low cost to serve a customer of $39, which provides them with an enviable operating leverage.

At the same time, customers are encouraged to open a checking account with Dave so they can get cash instantly and earn an APY of 4% on their deposits, while FDIC-insured Evolve Bank holds them. This allows them to issue Mastercard debit cards, which help them gain interchange revenue for every purchase.

Q1 Earnings

On Thursday, Dave announced Q1 earnings, which is why the stock is up 58% over the past three trading days, and the main reason why the stock is overvalued. There, the company announced quarterly revenues that exhibited a double beat with revenue of $108 million, coming $14.4 million higher than expected, and normalized EPS of $2.48, substantially beating earnings by $1.70. Yet, similar to Sezzle (SEZL), as commented in my previous analysis about them, the main stock boost was derived from a guidance revision that raised the outlook substantially.

Overall, the business's unit economics improved. In Q1, they achieved $1.531 billion in originations, representing a substantial rise of 46% from the prior year. This was obtained by a rise in total members to 12.4 million and a higher average loan size. Simultaneously, 28-day delinquency rates substantially dropped from 1.83% to 1.50%, reflecting a strong credit performance.

Previously, Dave's fee structure when obtaining a loan was based on voluntary tips and additional instant transfer fees. Recently, in Q1, the company introduced a new fee structure that is based on a higher loan of $5 or 5% of the total loan, where instant transfer fees to Dave's checking accounts are free. This new model has allowed them to raise the average revenue loan from $9.0 a year ago to $11.4, representing a QoQ rise of 12%, which is solid even when considering that the full benefit is expected to materialize in Q2.

At the same time, the rise in total members has brought tailwinds to the interchange business, with debit card spend volumes rising to $488 million, representing a 24% YoY growth.

Guidance

As commented, the highlight of the earnings results was the guidance adjustments, which consist of expected revenue in FY 2025 at a mid-point of $467.5 million, which represents a rise of 35%. In addition, the adjusted EBITDA was raised substantially, where the company expects to grow at a mid-point of 85%, which implies an adjusted net income margin of 34%, a 900 bps improvement from the 25% margin of FY 2024.

Valuation

With the stock price recovery, especially after Liberation Day and Q1 post earnings, Dave's forward PE valuation has drastically moved higher to the point that it is far away from its historical average, trading at 21.6x forward PE. Not so long ago, in April, Dave's valuation was just 8.3x, implying that the stock had become more than 2.6x more expensive in over a month. Despite the company expecting to grow revenues at a mid-point of 35% in FY 2025, the high valuation is a red flag, and from a technical perspective, an entry point on the stock at $182 is not attractive.

The business could be doing great, growing fast, providing solid guidance, but a 21.6x forward PE valuation is too high for a lending firm, and too risky to start a position when the stock valuation appreciation was parabolic, as exhibited in the chart above. For example, out of the 34 companies in the Consumer Finance industry, Dave is the third one with the highest forward PE valuation after SoFi Technologies (SOFI) and Upstart Holdings (UPST). Overall, the median valuation of the industry is 9.5x price-to-forward PE, and Dave started trading 126.9% higher than the industry, in a short period of time.

Risks

The outlook provided by the company is completely opposite to the macro uncertainty that led to the negative GDP growth in Q1 and could lead to higher consumer delinquencies on loans going forward. Nonetheless, investment banks such as JPMorgan (JPM) and Goldman Sachs (GS) have dialed back from their recession forecast in 2025, implying probabilities of less than 50%, due to a trade deal with China in which tariffs were reduced significantly for a period of 90 days. However, the appreciation of 58% after liberation day suggests that the market was pricing a guaranteed soft landing.

Takeaway

The unit economics of Dave are great, and it is a growth company with a solid net income margin of 13.75%. The macro situation with regard to uncertainty from tariff effects has eased, and that could be beneficial to the credit quality of financial institutions. At the same time, the outlook for FY 2025 was pretty strong, which is the main reason why the stock rose substantially after posting Q1 earnings. Nonetheless, the valuation has drastically become overvalued at a forward PE of 21.6x. In situations like this, with parabolic price moves, the best course of action is to take profits, as most of the gains have probably been achieved already. Considering all of this, I will rate the stock of Dave as a "sell."

data.title: FirstCash Holdings: A Business Built To Last Through Good Times And Bad
data.description:
Introduction

With so much uncertainty in the US stock market as of late, I recently wanted to find some new potential holdings that wouldn't just survive in a volatile macro regime, but potentially thrive. That was what led me to FirstCash Holdings (FCFS), the largest operator of pawn stores in the United States.

While the idea of a pawn shop being a strong recession resistant idea is not earth-shattering, I was admittedly very impressed when I dived into their latest earnings report and historical performance. With the stock recently breaking out to new all-time highs while the S&P 500 still recovering from a steep April selloff, I believe the setup is still attractive and was enough for me to initiate a small starter position in the stock.

In this article, I want to explore my investment thesis and explain why I believe the company has a good chance to continue delivering strong results, despite an uncertain yet cautiously optimistic outlook for the market at large.

Company Overview

Headquartered in Fort Worth, TX, FCFS operates over 3,000 pawn locations in the U.S. and Latin America. The current state of the company was largely formed in 2016 through a merger between First Cash Financial Services and Cash America Inc., while a 2021 acquisition of American First Finance marked an entry by the company into the buy now, pay later space. In addition to stores across 29 US states, the company has stores in Mexico, Guatemala, Colombia, and El Salvador across dozens of different store brand concepts.

In a recessionary or stressed economic environment, pawn loans can behave like countercyclical income streams, and FirstCash has proven this out over multiple cycles. The current trade and currency backdrop only makes the story more compelling.

Investment Thesis: The Pawn Model is Lucrative and Countercyclical; the Stock Trades Defensively

The simplicity of the pawn business model is what makes it so powerful in volatile economic environments. A customer brings in a physical asset—jewelry, electronics, tools—and receives a short-term loan secured by that item. If the loan is repaid, the customer gets their item back. If not, the pawn store sells the item to recover the loan principal and interest. There’s no unsecured lending risk, no dependence on credit scores, and minimal exposure to default.

What makes this model inherently counter-cyclical is its alignment with economic stress. When traditional credit tightens or when household liquidity gets squeezed, more people turn to pawn shops. Demand for secured loans rises, and inventory naturally flows into the retail side of the business. The result is potentially higher loan volume, faster inventory turnover, and pricing power in both lending rates and resale margins. It’s a rare retail model that can grow both sides of its P&L when consumers are under pressure. That being said, it doesn't rely on a poor economy to produce strong financial results, as the strong growth of the company over the last 10 years has evidenced.

Better said, FCFS is a well managed business with strong unit economics that has compounded earnings in good times and bad, having recently posted exceptional results right at a time when consumers are becoming more pessimistic. With strong net margins, the pawn model proves to be much more lucrative than traditional big box retailers like Target (TGT), Walmart (WMT) or even superstar Costco (COST). This largely flies under the radar due to the company being classed as a Financial rather than a Retail company, although I believe it is appropriate to compare to both sectors when analyzing. It is this grey area that might explain why the company is not a household name along with its relative small size ($6B market cap).

Another interesting thing to note on the company is its strong track record of value accretive acquisitions, which has been done at both a small regular scale of acquiring single stores (one store in Las Vegas was purchased in Q1, for example) and large-scale acquisitions like the 2016 merger or the recently announced deal to buy H&T Group, the leading Pawn operator in the United Kingdom. When looking at the financials, it can be seen that the company uses cash on hand to make small acquisitions and open new stores, while they fund large acquisitions of entire corporate entities through a blend of equity and debt. This is why Gross Margins peaked in 2002 and Return on Equity has swung up and down wildly over the years. While the company has been quite aggressive in making acquisitions, the fact that ROE and Gross Margins have been ticking up since the 2016 merger indicates to me that the management team understands how to integrate new concepts in a way that ultimately creates shareholder value. The deal aggressive deal to acquire H&T is the latest piece of evidence that the FCFS growth story still has a long way to run, potentially.

Solid Financial Results and Track Record Not Fully Realized in the Valuation

The Q1 report was clean. EPS came in at $1.86, up 39% year-over-year. Revenue grew over 14%, with double-digit growth in both U.S. and Latin American segments. Same-store pawn loans increased 13% in the U.S. and 14% in Latin America in constant currency, and fee income growth was strong across geographies.

The margins remained intact. U.S. segment operating margins were 23.9%, and Latin America saw margin expansion to 26.7%. Retail sales margins also improved modestly, and the merchandise mix continues to tilt toward high-turnover electronics and gold.

When the most recent quarterly numbers are juxtaposed against the annual numbers over the last 10 years, it doesn't look as extraordinary, as the company has increased Gross Profits by more than 5 times since 2015, faster than the ~4.5x increase in revenue seen over the same period. Indeed, the gross profit expansion has not been linear when accounting for the large M&A deals the company has done over this time, but has edged up to new local highs in subsequent years each time, showing strong integration capabilities.

Despite very strong financial metrics and an all-time high stock price, the current valuation is still below 5 year averages, making it look highly attractive.

Currency and Tariff Setup: Peso Strength Is Already Showing Up

While not very important to our thesis in the immediate sense, FirstCash earns a large share of revenue and profits in Mexico, and guidance assumes currency neutrality and a prolonged period of further dollar strength. However, I actually believe that Mexico is a big beneficiary of trade tension with China and the Mexican Peso has appreciated meaningfully vs. the dollar both over the last 5 years and this QTD. If the peso continues to strengthen structurally over time, the company will benefit without having to raise expectations. While this isn't necessary to underpin a strong investment thesis, it is a nice bonus and a way for US-based investors to get exposure to Mexico and Latin America.

Secondly, FirstCash is not materially exposed to Chinese imports or tariff risk. While the tariff outlook is changing daily and the markets currently lit with enthusiasm of easing trade tensions, I am taking a more skeptical approach with my portfolio. The company's product flow is heavily skewed toward refurbished consumer electronics and secondhand gold jewelry, items that are largely sourced domestically or within the local region and sometimes at negative acquisition costs if acquired due to defaulted pawn loans. That makes FCFS one of the few retail-adjacent names with zero exposure to the tariff debate, at least in terms of first-order effects. If anything, if new merchandise recently imported gets marked up aggressively due to tariffs, FirstCash might actually benefit from the "trade-down" effect that commonly benefits Costco but in a differentiated manner.

In a macro environment where trade barriers are rising, the dollar is weakening, and consumer liquidity is tightening, FirstCash stands out as a structurally advantaged model with resilient features. Furthermore, with management continuously compounding cashflow into more locations and returning cash to shareholders, there is little reason to believe that the company doesn't continue to generate market-beating rates of return especially given its modest valuation multiple of less than 20 times, lower than the average S&P 500 company.

Risks

Investing in FCFS comes with several risks. One that sticks out is the acquisitive nature of the management team as discussed both historically and currently. While there is strong historical precedent for these acquisitions ultimately accreting shareholder value, integration risk is always a concern, particularly if the company relies heavily on issuing shares to help fund them. This risk is seen as a double-edged sword in my view as it can be the ultimate driver of shareholder value in the long term but cause financial swings in the interim.

Another risk that is pronounced through the operations in South and Central America and the planned acquisition of the UK business is currency risk. Earlier I mentioned that the near and intermediate term impact of this might be neutral or even positive given the current environment of potential pervasive dollar weakness, however it is not easy to predict currency moves. The company turning into more of a multinational will amplify the currency risk over time, but again this serves as a double-edged sword that can insulate and diversify US shareholders without having to directly hold foreign currency.

Lastly, another risk that was touched on earlier vaguely is that at least over the past year, the stock has traded with modest inverse correlation to the S&P 500 on a day-to-day basis. While the stock has still trended up over time, it is reasonable to expect that an investment in this stock will not merely perform as beta to the index but rather a defensive but compounding position. I believe that this risk is core to the investment thesis however and can drive material outperformance of the index, so I feel it as fairly benign or at least a risk I am willing and eager to take on.

All in all, the company presents risks that aren't too atypical from a small-cap growth stock. However, its "defensive compounder" properties do still make the risks associated with it a good fit in contrast with the rest of my portfolio.

Technical Analysis

The stock has recently broken out to new all-time highs, ultimately resulting in a very bullish looking chart. Perhaps more interestingly, the stock price has been an inverse mirror image of the S&P 500 over the last year, swing trading in the opposite direction for the majority of the time. The combination of these two anecdotal datapoints make the current chart look like a truly excellent setup to enter, despite the current price not screaming full-tilt value. While the stock has recently traded sluggishly with the broader market ripping on trade optimism, this behavior falls in line with the stock trading as a defensive safe haven. With such a strong track record for growth, it truly looks like a great technical entry point for this defensive compounder.

Why I Started a Position

This is one of the rare businesses where the operating model is directly aligned with the kind of macro stress I think we may be heading into. While a lot of the defensive positioning I have done over the last month has underperformed the market on trade optimism the last 2 weeks, I feel confident in doubling down on more uncertainty throughout the year here.

FirstCash is set up to escape the trade war noise and continue to attract investment dollars. Add in strong unit economics, real capital discipline, and a historically recession-resilient model, and I’m comfortable initiating a small position even after a strong YTD move.

I’ll consider sizing into it more aggressively if we get a pullback since this is one of my smallest positions currently, but I’m now watching this name much more closely.

Final Thoughts: Worth a Flier Position; Buy

FirstCash doesn’t get much attention, and that’s probably part of the appeal. It’s a simple business, but one that can thrive in complex environments. With earnings strength already showing up, macro tailwinds forming, and the stock still reasonably valued on forward earnings, I think the setup deserves a closer look.

data.title: Lundbeck Delivering On The Financials, But Needs Some Positive Clinical Drivers
data.description:
The last few months have not been the best for the shares of Danish drugmaker H. Lundbeck A/S (OTCPK:HLBBF) (OTCPK:HLUBF) (HLUNa.CO) (HLUNb.CO), as concerns about tariffs and challenges to prices in the U.S., coupled with a disappointing clinical update, have combined to drive a roughly 15% decline in the local share price since my last update, a little worse than the performance of the broader European pharmaceutical sector (as measured by the STOXX Europe Total Market Pharmaceuticals index).

Lundbeck is in a challenging place right now. While the underlying fundamental performance of the business is pretty good, there are legitimate concerns about what the U.S. administration’s new policies could mean for the company, and there won’t likely be any significant clinical read-outs until late in the year. The pipeline does support worthwhile upside from here, but this is a stock where investor worries about drivers outside of management’s control could outweigh positive financial results for a few more quarters.

Good Momentum In Q1

In terms of the here-and-now financial performance at Lundbeck, I can’t find many complaints. The company followed up better-than-expected Q4 ’24 results with Q1 '25 results that were well ahead of expectations and didn’t really have any concerning flaws. While the guidance boost may disappoint some (particularly given the magnitude of the beat), I think conservative guidance is prudent at this point.

Revenue rose 16% in local currencies, beating expectations by about 8%. Growth was driven by the company’s “Strategic Brands”, which grew 24% (9% better than expected), while the company’s legacy business saw 2% revenue erosion (still good for a 6% beat).

Vyepti, the company’s migraine drug, was the leader in terms of growth (up 62%, making up about 17% of total revenue) and performance relative to expectations (beating by 17%). Rexulti (up 28%, beating by 5%) remains the largest overall contributor to revenue (around 24%), while the Abilify franchise (up 16%, beating by 11% and making up 16% of revenue) and Trintellix (up 7%, beating by 6%, and making up 20% of revenue) did fine. Lexapro/Cipralex, long off patent in most major markets, saw 1% growth in the quarter and still contributes about 10% of total revenue.

Gross margin improved 170bp year over year to 82.6%, beating by 50bp, helped by scale benefits and lower amortization costs. Operating expenses rose 13% in local currencies, a little higher than expected, but the beat on gross margin nevertheless allowed for 33% growth in operating income (margin up three points to 27.2%) and 24% local currency growth in adjusted EBITDA (margin up 190bp to 34.9%), beating by 21%.

As I said, the revision to guidance was modest relative to the beat versus expectations this quarter. Management raised its revenue growth guide from 7%-10% to 8%-11%, while boosting the adjusted EBITDA growth guide from 5%-11% to 8%-14%. Variability in ordering patterns (stocking/de-stocking), as well as impending patent expirations for some products in some geographies seem to be the primary reasons for relatively restrained guidance at this point.

Rexulti Performing Well, But An Upcoming AdComm Remains An Important Catalyst

Just after the publication of my last article, Lundbeck announced that the FDA made a late decision to hold an advisory committee meeting to review the company’s application for FDA approval to add the post-traumatic stress disorder (or PTSD) indication to the Rexulti label (in combination with sertraline, an already-approved drug). With the Q1'25 release, we now know that the panel meeting will be held relatively soon - on July 18, 2025.

As has been the case for drug development for PTSD in general, Rexulti’s path so far has had its ups and downs, with the pivotal Phase III results seeing the fixed dose miss its primary endpoint, while the flexible dose met it.

While a well-designed study should help Lundbeck’s case, it’s tough to call this more than a 50–50 shot at this point. It’s true that PTSD is a badly underserved condition in the U.S. - there are around 13M people diagnosed with the condition and close to 2M being treated with pharmaceuticals for the condition - but it’s also true that the FDA has been unpredictable when it comes to drugs with mixed clinical data.

Assuming no major changes to Rexulti pricing, 5% share of the U.S. PTSD drug-treated patient market could be worth something on the order of $1.5B/year in revenue. Given that Rexulti has already grown to around 3% share of the U.S. Alzheimer’s-associated agitation market two years after approval (and that indication now makes up about 20% of Rexulti revenue), I do think there is solid potential for the PTSD indication to add $1B or more to Rexulti’s annual sales contributions in the coming years, provided of course that the FDA approves the application.

Clinical And Operational Unknowns Are Certainly Risks

Lundbeck’s pipeline isn’t in the best shape, with a sparse late-stage pipeline and elevated clinical risk, but the three drugs it does have in Phase II/III testing do all have meaningful financial potential for the company and shareholders.

Amlenetug started its first Phase III study (MASCOT) late in 2024 despite a mixed Phase II outcome (“trends” and “signals” of efficacy, but a failure on the primary endpoint), and the drug has received orphan drug designations in the U.S., EU, and Japan. Multiple system atrophy (or MSA), the lead indication for the drug today and the focus of the MASCOT study, is an awful disease with no treatments today and could become a multibillion-dollar drug near the end of the decade, but I’m always nervous when Phase II studies produce “yes, but…” results.

Lundbeck is also developing Lu AG09222 as a new treatment for migraine, and announced back in March that an interim analysis of its Phase II PROCEED study led the company to pursue new IV formulation dose-response information after finishing the subcutaneous portion of the study. It looks as though the Street has interpreted this to mean that the sub-q formulation hasn’t delivered the hoped-for results, and I believe this is part of the reason why the stock has been weak – while Vyepti has shown that there is a market for IV-administered treatments for migraine, the IV administration requirement definitely limits its market potential.

Last but not least is bexicaserin, which the company is developing for Developmental and Epileptic Encephalopathies (or DEEs), an umbrella term for a host of conditions that cause seizures. Early results from a small Phase Ib/IIa study have been encouraging, with greater than 50% seizure frequency reduction, and the market potential is meaningful ($1B-plus), but this is an area of drug development littered with late-stage failures, and it remains a high-risk program in my mind.

Beyond the clinical risk here, there are also potential operational risks. As I’ve said in the past, positive clinical outcomes may happen, but the expiration of patents on key drugs like Abilify Maintena, Trintellix, and Rexulti, will happen in the coming years, and management will have to navigate the impact those have on sales and margins, particularly as the company cannot cut back too far lest it risk endangering the market potential of its pipeline.

There’s also the more recent issue of tariffs and potential price controls in the United States. It remains to be seen what the administration can actually do with respect to drug prices without Congress’s involvement, but it is at the very least a threat given that the company generates about half of its revenue in the U.S.

The actual impact to Lundbeck is hard to gauge at this point, as the price discrepancies between the U.S. and Europe differ by drug (with a large difference for Rexulti, but less for drugs like Vyepti) as well as by market channel (the Alzheimer’s agitation indication for Rexulti, for instance, is more weighted to government-funded plans than the depression indication).

The Outlook

Not too much has changed in my model relative to my last update, as financial outperformance has been counterbalanced by what I expect will be increased operating spending in the coming years to support R&D and to maximize revenue (more spending on sales and marketing, in other words).

Summing together the next five years’ revenue, my estimates have changed less than 1%, and I’m looking for roughly flat revenue over the next five years as growth in franchises like Vyepti is offset by patent expirations. FDA approval for, and meaningful adoption of, Rexulti in PTSD would certainly offer some upside.

My long-term revenue growth estimate is likewise pretty flattish, largely because I use a risk discounting mechanism for pipeline candidates – in other words, if and when the clinical trials for drugs in the pipeline offer positive read-outs (increasing the likelihood of eventual approval/commercialization), the expected revenue contributions will rise. There are, of course, no guarantees here, and key pipeline failures would likely lead the company to pursue additional M&A/in-licensing to preserve revenue.

As far as margins go, I think management’s target of 30%-plus adjusted EBITDA margin over the next five years is attainable, and I’m expecting free cash flow margins in the high-teens, with upside into the low-20%’s over time. That should drive low single-digit FCF growth even on flattish revenues, with upside if and when the pipeline candidates prove out.

Between discounted free cash flow and a P/E approach driven off of what the market has historically paid for a given level of EPS growth, I believe Lundbeck shares are undervalued today, potentially by more than 35%. The major downside risks at this point would be clinical trial failures and the imposition of price controls in the U.S. that would meaningfully impact revenue.

The Bottom-Line

I do believe Lundbeck shares are once again too cheap, but I also believe that the shares need to see positive regulatory and clinical developments to get moving up again in a sustained way. A successful AdComm meeting for the PTSD indication for Rexulti would certainly help, but otherwise investors are going to have to wait a bit for thesis-altering clinical data. In the meantime, good financial performance will certainly help, but this is a name that’s going to require patience to work out.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Government Bond Yields Extend Climb Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
The benchmark 10-year US Treasury yield climbed above 4.5% this week for the first time in nearly three months, as tariff threats, inflation fears and higher-for-longer interest rates triggered another sell-off in US government bonds.

The Treasury 10-year yield, which moves opposite prices, has risen about 50 basis points since President Donald Trump's April 2 announcement revealing steep tariffs on goods from nearly all the country's trading partners.

Government bond yields since early April have been on "the tariff roller coaster," said Padhraic Garvey, head of global rates and debt strategy at ING, as tariffs have been delayed, threats by Trump have been walked back and some details of preliminary trade talks have bolstered hopes of a trade policy reprieve.

But with the Federal Reserve unlikely to move on rates until the tariff impact starts showing up in government data, the bond market is largely anticipating the sell-off to continue amidst heightened fears of higher inflation and interest rates. The 10-year yield could soon near 5% for the first time since October 2023.

"I'd suggest there is a general tolerance this side of 5%," Garvey said in an interview. "We've been at 5% before in this cycle, so getting back there would not be a dramatic move. That said, shooting above 5% could indeed be problematic, and likely only tolerable if the economy is doing really well."

Much of the ongoing sell-off in government bonds has taken place in longer term yields. The 30-year yield, for example, fell by about 30 basis points from the start of Trump's second presidential term in January to his April 2 tariff announcement, but has climbed by about 40 bps since. The 20-year yield, which initially fell 33 bps, has climbed 42 bps since April 2.

Tariffs may not ultimately prove as inflationary as markets currently fear, according to John Luke Tyner, head of fixed income and portfolio manager at Aptus Capital Advisors. Yields will ultimately be driven by economic growth, with rate likely pushing higher if the US does not appear to be imminently entering a recession, and inflation expectations as the market adjusts for future inflation risks.

"I would think this generally should keep term premiums elevated," Tyner said in an interview.

Where the bond market is headed will hinge on how much the tariffs actually stick and for how long, as this will impact the future direction of inflation, the labor market and the Fed's decision on when to cut rates and by how much, said Patrick Leary, managing director with Loop Capital Markets.

"We will need to wait and see what develops, but in the meantime, fixed income investors are favoring the front end and belly of the yield curve and avoiding the long end," Leary said in an interview. "This is a sharp contrast to previous recession fear trades."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Near Double-Digit Yield Likely Secure, But Wait For Now
data.description:
Introduction

As a business development company ("BDC") enthusiast, I'm currently still holding my BDCs and will continue to do so, despite a potential recession in the coming months.

However, I do think some will continue covering their base dividends, but are likely to see downside as a result of expected lower interest rates.

Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (NYSE:BXSL), one of the top BDCs that could perform solidly in a potential recession, reported their latest earnings and their performance could be an indication of what could be coming.

In this article, I discuss Blackstone Secured Lending's latest earnings report, fundamentals, and why I'm re-iterating my hold rating as they could see further downside despite their strong fundamentals.

Previous Hold Rating

I last covered Blackstone Secured Lending this past March, downgrading the BDC from a buy to a hold due to heightened uncertainty. With recession fears picking up as a result of a potential trade war, I assumed BXSL's upside was limited.

Since then, their share price has held up relatively well, but is still down vs the overall market. BXSL is down close to 6% compared to the S&P (SP500), up 4.51% at the time of writing.

During Q4, the BDC reported a strong quarter in terms of investment commitments over $1 billion and fundings totaling $1.4 billion.

Net investment income missed analysts' estimates by $0.02 and dropped $0.07 from the previous quarter, amounting to $0.84.

Total investment income managed to grow a solid $10 million from the previous quarter and $49 million the previous year.

BXSL also had a solid balance sheet with leverage below the peer average and robust liquidity totaling $2.4 billion. They also received full investment-grade credit ratings from Fitch & Moody's last year as a result.

Lower Base Rates, An Obvious Headwind

As previously mentioned, I plan to continue to hold my current BDCs, but the macro environment is likely to drag their share prices lower.

So, for investors looking to buy since many have pulled back recently, you could potentially get a better entry point over the next 6 to 7 months.

Reason being the economy is seemingly starting to show cracks. Recently, GDP declined for the first time since 2022, an obvious sign that consumer spending is slowing.

Moreover, this would also likely lead to compressed spreads for BDCs and lower investment activity, especially if a recession comes to fruition like many are assuming.

The overall market reacted positively to news of a potential trade deal with the U.K. recently. Shortly after, the market jumped another 1,000 points due to a potential deal with China that saw both countries reduce tariffs for 90 days.

And while trade deals in the future could avoid us falling into a recession, negative growth in the upcoming quarter would mean a recession, by definition standards.

As a result, the FED would likely lower interest rates. Currently, the market is pricing in interest rates to be between 50 basis points lower by December of this year.

Moreover, with the recent report showing CPI cooling more than expected, this could be the green light the FED needs to lower base rates further than expected. Ultimately, meaning lower portfolio yields and interest income for BDCs.

And this would likely result in lower share prices as investors potentially flee as BDCs become less attractive.

On the flip side, future tariffs would mean potentially higher inflation, a positive for the sector. And this could be a reason the FED kept interest rates steady at their recent meeting, as they continue to monitor incoming data.

Tariff impacts could be short-lived, but slowing economic growth could force the FED's hand. The economy has continued to be somewhat resilient, but I think we see at least one cut this year.

However, this would still have a negative impact on BDCs due to their predominantly floating-rate portfolios. But during BXSL's recent Q1 earnings, the slowing economy and base rates 100 basis points lower are already having an impact.

Net investment income of $0.83 managed to beat estimates by a penny, while total investment income was in-line with the consensus at $358 million. This grew solidly from the previous quarter's $353 million and from $304 million year-over-year.

This was likely attributed to overall portfolio growth, which grew impressively from the previous year. BXSL's portfolio value stood at $12.8 billion, up from $10.4 billion in the previous year's quarter.

Borrowers grew from 210 companies to 284. During the quarter, they added 14 new borrowers while exiting six.

And while BXSL's portfolio growth is to be commended, especially considering the current macro environment, their overall yield and investments/fundings declined from the previous year and quarter, an obvious result of lower base rates and a slowing economy.

Their yield was 10.2%, down from 10.4% in Q4 and 11.8% in Q1'24. This is also likely the reason their bottom line declined from $0.84 in the prior quarter and $0.87 annually.

As a result, dividend coverage dropped from 113% to 108%, still solid in comparison to some of their larger peers. However, this is something investors should keep a close eye on. To be fair, of its peer group, BXSL had the highest coverage with the exception of Ares Capital's (ARCC) 112.5%.

Investments & fundings also dropped below $1 billion, breaking Blackstone Secured Lending's streak of 5 consecutive quarters seeing more than $1 billion. In Q1, commitments amounted to $756 million, down from $1.24 billion in Q4 and $1.17 billion year-over-year.

Fundings also retracted from $1.38 billion in the previous quarter to $689 million. But management stated they expect these to pick up in the back half of the year.

But if the economy continues to slow, which, I think, could be likely, these could continue to decline, impacting their financials and dividend coverage in the coming quarters.

However, with BXSL's investment-grade balance sheet, high first-lien exposure, robust liquidity, larger borrowers, and manageable non-accruals, I suspect they will maintain and continue paying their current dividend of $0.77.

Leverage crept during the quarter, in-line with the peer average of 1.19x. But this was still within management's targeted range of 1.0x - 1.25x.

Liquidity was plentiful, with $3.4 billion in cash & revolver capacity and $1.3 billion in total debt maturing in January & September of next year.

Non-accruals rose slightly year-over-year on a cost basis from 0.1% to 0.3% and were flat at 0.1% at fair value. But it was well below the peer average of 2.7% and 1.2% on a cost & fair value basis according to BXSL's management during the quarter.

The LTM weighted-average borrower EBITDA in the latest quarter stood at $210 million, up nearly 10% from the previous year's $193 million.

While larger borrowers don't necessarily equate to better performances in downturns, Blackstone Secured Lending Fund's steady non-accruals seem to be a testament to this and their overall credit quality.

Valuation

At the current price of $30.62 at the time of writing, Blackstone Secured Lending is much more attractive than they have been in the last few years with higher for longer interest rates. At the current price, this gives them a price-to-NAV ratio of 1.13x.

Here's how they compare to peers:

Ares Capital: 1.06x

Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC): 0.91x

FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK): 0.84x.

Currently, BXSL & ARCC trade at slight premiums to their NAVs while OBDC & FSK appear more attractive currently trading at discounts. The current 8.4% premium is slightly higher than their 3-year average of 5.03%.

And while I think BXSL's fundamentals command a premium, limited upside and headwinds from potential lower base rates makes them a hold as further downside is likely.

I expect BXSL to trade near the $30 range for the near-term, but they could see selling pressure in the latter half of the year, potentially creating a better entry point.

Risks & Bottom Line

As mentioned previously, lower yields from lower base rates will continue to put pressure on BXSL's dividend coverage. In turn, this will likely increase selling pressure as investors become worried about a potential dividend cut.

But due to their strong fundamentals and robust liquidity, I suspect Blackstone Secured Lending will maintain their current $0.77 dividend, at least for the near to medium-term.

Investors should continue to monitor non-accruals and net investment income in the coming quarters, as these could become impacted if the economy continues to weaken.

While a falling share price below NAV would likely prompt me to upgrade Blackstone Secured Lending Fund to buy, increased economic uncertainty and expected lower base rates keeps me at a hold for now.
